name: Italian Game
description: One of the oldest and most respected openings. White develops the bishop to c4, targeting the weak f7 pawn.

moves.commentary: Central pawn advance. This move controls the center and opens lines for your queen and bishop.

children.commentary: Black mirrors with e5, establishing a classical pawn center. The position is now symmetrical.
Nf3 develops the knight with tempo, attacking e5 and preparing to castle kingside.
Nc6 defends e5 and develops. This leads to the Italian Game proper.
Bc4 targets the weak f7 square. The Italian Game - White's bishop eyes the vulnerable pawn.
Bc5 - the Giuoco Piano. Black develops the bishop to an active square.
c3 prepares d4 to challenge the center and support the e4 pawn.
Nc3 develops the knight and creates tension in the center.
The Evans Gambit sacrifices a pawn for rapid development and attack.

defenses.name: Giuoco Piano
defenses.profile: The Giuoco Piano (Italian: 'quiet game') is the most classical response to the Italian Game. Black develops the bishop to c5, mirroring White's development and preparing to castle.

tree.commentary: Bc5 - the Giuoco Piano. Black develops the bishop to an active square.

children.commentary: c3 prepares d4 to challenge the center.

defenses.name: Two Knights Defense
defenses.profile: The Two Knights Defense attacks e4 immediately. Black gets active play.

defenses.name: Hungarian Defense
defenses.profile: The Hungarian Defense is a solid response.

strategy.keyIdea: Develop pieces toward the center and target the vulnerable f7 pawn before the opponent can fully consolidate.
strategy.middleGamePlan: After castling kingside, aim to build a strong pawn center with c3 and d4. Coordinate your bishop on c4 with your knights to create pressure on the kingside. The position rewards patient maneuvering over early fireworks.
strategy.watchOut: Rushing an attack before completing development — the Italian rewards preparation, and premature aggression often backfires.
typicalGoals: Castle kingside early to connect rooks
Play c3 followed by d4 to challenge the center
Keep your bishop on c4 active and pointed at f7
Coordinate knights toward outpost squares on d5 or f5

quiz.question: You've just finished the Italian Game opening. What's your primary middlegame goal?

options.text: Launch an immediate kingside pawn storm with f4-f5
options.explanation: The Italian is a positional opening — you need to finish development and castle before attacking.

options.text: Play c3 and d4 to establish a strong pawn center
options.explanation: Correct. The c3-d4 break is the Italian's main strategic threat, seizing central space and activating your pieces.

options.text: Trade your bishop for the opponent's knight to simplify
options.explanation: Your bishop on c4 is one of your best pieces in the Italian — keep it active and pointed at f7.

default.opponentMoveSummary: Your opponent stepped off the main theory line with an unusual move, choosing their own path rather than the expected response.
default.whyBookBreaks: The standard line assumes a particular pawn or piece configuration that this move bypasses.
default.opponentStrategy: Your opponent is trying to reach a less-charted position where their preparation or intuition gives them an edge.
forwardGuidance: Prioritize completing your development — get all minor pieces off the back rank.
Control the center with pawns or piece pressure before launching any flank operations.

d6.opponentMoveSummary: Black played d6, supporting the e5 pawn and preparing a solid but passive setup.
d6.whyBookBreaks: The Italian main lines assume Black will develop actively with Bc5 or Nf6. d6 avoids the sharp Giuoco Piano and Two Knights entirely.
d6.opponentStrategy: Hungarian Defense style — Black chooses solidity over activity, avoiding sharp tactical lines in favor of a slower, more cramped game.
forwardGuidance: Play d4 when ready to challenge the center — Black's passive setup gives you space to expand.
Develop your pieces actively; Black's d6-e5 chain can become a target for piece pressure.

Be7.opponentMoveSummary: Black played Be7, developing the bishop to a solid square instead of the active c5.
Be7.whyBookBreaks: The main Italian lines expect Black's bishop on c5 (Giuoco Piano) or the knight on f6 (Two Knights). Be7 sidesteps both.
Be7.opponentStrategy: Paris Defense — Black develops solidly to e7, avoiding sharp theory and aiming for a quiet, positional game.
forwardGuidance: Complete development and castle — you retain a small space advantage.
Consider d4 or d3 to control the center; Black's passive bishop gives you time.

Nc6.opponentMoveSummary: Black played Nc6, developing the knight to defend e5 and control d4.
Nc6.whyBookBreaks: At this move order, the book may expect a different response. Nc6 leads to the Italian proper or can transpose to other lines.
Nc6.opponentStrategy: Black opts for classical development, aiming for a standard Italian or Ruy Lopez–style position.
forwardGuidance: Continue with Bc4 or Bb5 depending on your preferred line — both are strong.
Control the center and complete kingside development before committing to a plan.
